selected_skills.trigger_context: Use when a PR modifies admin or privileged backend/API routes, route
selected_skills.trait_instruction: Use when a PR modifies admin or privileged backend/API routes, route

focus_areas: Authentication and authorization on backend routes
specific_checks: Verify the new /posts route is actually exported/mounted; the file currently defines a router but does not export it.
Check whether /posts should require authentication or role-based access before exposing post and author data.
Review whether attaching full user records as post.author may leak sensitive fields from db.users.findById results.
Confirm error handling exists for database failures and missing authors rather than allowing unhandled promise rejections or partial responses.

focus_areas: Async route behavior and response correctness
specific_checks: Flag the per-post await db.users.findById(post.authorId) loop as a likely N+1 query pattern; recommend batching or joining authors.
Check whether mutating ORM/entity objects returned by db.posts.findAll() is safe and intended.
Verify the route handler handles large post sets without serial author lookups causing latency spikes.
Ensure async database calls are wrapped so failures return controlled HTTP errors instead of hanging or crashing the request.
Confirm src/routes/posts.ts follows the project's route-module conventions used by other files in src/routes.
Verify the router is exported so it can be mounted from src/index.ts or the central router setup.
Check whether enriching posts with author objects changes the expected API contract and whether tests should be added or updated.
Recommend adding route-level tests for GET /posts covering success, empty results, and database error cases.

focus_areas: Express route module completeness and mounting
Authentication and authorization on backend routes
API contract and response schema stability
specific_checks: Verify the new /posts route is exported and mounted according to the project's existing src/routes conventions.
Check whether GET /posts should require authentication or role-based authorization before exposing post and author data.
Review whether attaching author objects to posts may leak sensitive user fields from db.users.findById results; ensure only safe fields are returned.
Confirm the response shape matches intended API contracts and recommend updating or adding tests if enriching posts changes the schema.

risk_profile: Sensitive data leakage via nested author objects

focus_areas: Async route behavior and response correctness
specific_checks: Flag the per-post await db.users.findById(post.authorId) loop as a likely N+1 query pattern; recommend batching, eager loading, or joining authors.
Check whether mutating ORM/entity objects returned by db.posts.findAll() is safe and intended versus mapping to plain response objects.
Verify the route handles large post sets without serial author lookups causing latency spikes.
Ensure async database calls are wrapped so failures produce controlled HTTP responses instead of hanging or crashing requests.

hypothetical_bugs: The new GET /posts route may be incomplete or unreachable because the module never exports the router, and it may also not be mounted following existing src/routes conventions. This is a high-probability integration bug for a newly added Express route file.

GET /posts appears to expose post data and nested author data with no authentication or authorization middleware. If posts are not intended to be public, this creates an unintended public route exposure and possible privilege bypass.

Attaching db.users.findById(post.authorId) directly onto each post may leak sensitive user fields such as email, password hash, roles, or internal metadata, and it may also break the API contract by changing the response schema unexpectedly. Additionally, if findById returns null or throws, the handler currently has no controlled error handling, risking inconsistent responses or unhandled async failures.

N+1 query and serial await behavior in the `/posts` route: `db.posts.findAll()` is followed by a per-post `await db.users.findById(post.authorId)` loop, which will cause one additional query per post and execute them sequentially. Under large result sets this can create severe latency spikes and poor scalability.

Unhandled async/database failures in the route: there is no `try/catch` or error forwarding around `findAll()` or `findById()`. If either call rejects, the request may end in an unhandled promise rejection or Express error path instead of a controlled HTTP error response.

Unsafe mutation of ORM/entity instances returned from `db.posts.findAll()`: assigning `post.author = ...` directly may mutate persistence-layer objects or model instances in unintended ways, potentially causing serialization issues, dirty tracking side effects, or runtime errors if the returned objects are not meant to be extended.

iterations.final_synthesized_review_summary: 5 comments (Critical:1, High:4). This endpoint has several merge-blocking issues: it appears to be publicly accessible without auth, returns raw user records that may expose sensitive fields, lacks explicit async error forwarding, uses an N+1 author-loa
iterations.evaluator_comments: Strong review overall: it is specific to the route, actionable, and covers multiple categories well, especially sensitive-data exposure and the N+1 query issue. The suggested tests are also useful and concrete. However, several findings depend on assumptions not proven from the snippet, which hurts accuracy: missing auth may be intentional for a public posts endpoint, async error forwarding is only a real issue on Express 4 (not Express 5), and 'router not exported' cannot be concluded unless the full file/module context shows no export. Those should have been framed as conditional concerns rather than definite defects. The review would score higher if it separated confirmed issues from plausible risks and avoided overstating uncertain findings.
iterations.mutation_reason: Fitness 0.72 below target 0.90. removed_reviewers: Express Route Integration & API Security Reviewer

focus_areas: Confirmed route behavior visible in the diff
specific_checks: Only label an issue as a defect when the changed code itself proves it. If auth, export, mounting, or framework-version details are not shown, phrase them as conditional risks and explicitly state what evidence is missing.

Do not claim a route is unprotected unless the diff or surrounding file shows the absence of auth middleware where project conventions require it. If the endpoint may be intentionally public, say so and limit the finding to 'verify intended access control'.

Do not claim async error handling is broken unless the Express version or project wrapper pattern makes that true. If version is unknown, state: 'On Express 4 this needs try/catch/next; on Express 5 native async forwarding may already handle this.'

Do not claim the router is not exported or not mounted unless the file end or registration entrypoint is visible and confirms that. If export/mounting is not shown, ask for verification instead of filing a definite bug.

Never reference files, modules, middleware names, or helper APIs that are not present in the diff unless clearly marked as examples. Avoid hallucinated project paths or invented repository methods as if they already exist.

When suggesting fixes, separate 'minimal safe fix from shown code' from 'optional improvement if project supports X'. Do not prescribe `findByIds`, `requireAuth`, or specific registration files as existing facts unless shown.

Require line references to map to code actually implicated by the issue. Do not anchor findings to lines that only contain router setup when the real concern is response shaping or query logic elsewhere.
For every non-confirmed concern, include a verification step and the exact evidence needed to upgrade it to a confirmed defect.
risk_profile: Grounding failures from missing repository context
False positives about route exposure or mounting

specific_checks: Treat attaching raw `db.users.findById(...)` results to response objects as a confirmed data-exposure risk when the code returns the full user record without projection. This is a direct code-grounded issue.
Require fixes to project nested author data through an explicit allowlist of fields and to serialize plain response objects rather than mutating persistence-layer entities in place.

Call out API contract changes explicitly: adding `author` to each post changes response shape and may require versioning, client coordination, or contract tests. Phrase this as confirmed if the diff enriches the response.
Do not assume which fields are sensitive unless examples are framed as possibilities. State that any fields returned by the user repository beyond the intended public subset may leak.
Suggested tests must assert both positive and negative expectations: approved author fields are present, unapproved fields are absent, and the exact response shape is stable.
If recommending removal of nested author data, explain that this is an alternative product/API decision, not the only correctness fix.
Ensure line references point to the enrichment/serialization lines, not unrelated route declarations.
risk_profile: Sensitive data leakage via nested author objects

Do not prescribe a specific repository method such as `findByIds` as if it exists. Recommend batching/eager loading/joining generically, and if giving sample code, label missing APIs as illustrative.
Check whether the code mutates ORM/entity instances directly when attaching `author`; recommend mapping to plain objects if mutation semantics are uncertain.
Include correctness expectations for missing authors: reviewers should ask whether the intended contract is `author: null`, omission, or error, and tests should lock that behavior down.

Performance tests should verify bounded database-call counts or equivalent batching behavior, not just generic latency claims. Avoid unverifiable load-test demands unless the project already has that harness.
When discussing reliability, distinguish confirmed performance defects from conditional operational risks; do not overstate scalability conclusions beyond the visible serial query pattern.

Do not prescribe a specific repository method such as `findByIds` as if it exists. Recommend batching, eager loading, or joining generically; if giving sample code, label missing APIs as illustrative.

hypothetical_bugs: Potential route non-registration/export risk: this new router is created and populated, but the diff does not show any `export` from `src/routes/posts.ts` or any app/router mounting site. Do not file as confirmed from this diff alone; verify the file ends with a router export and that an entrypoint mounts it. Evidence needed: the remainder of this file and the route registration code.

Version-sensitive async error handling gap: the handler is `async` and awaits both `db.posts.findAll()` and `db.users.findById(...)` without any visible `try/catch` or `next(err)`. On Express 4, rejected promises here may bypass normal error middleware; on Express 5, native async forwarding may already handle this. Verify the Express major version or any project wrapper around async handlers before upgrading this to a confirmed defect.

Highly probable integration/performance edge case in the shown code: the loop performs one `db.users.findById` query per post, sequentially, creating an N+1 query pattern and slow responses for large post lists. Also verify behavior when `findById(post.authorId)` returns null/undefined, since the route mutates each post with `author` and may emit partially hydrated results. Evidence needed to confirm severity: expected post volume, DB API behavior, and whether response shape requires every author to exist.

Confirmed data-exposure risk in the response enrichment loop: `post.author = await db.users.findById(post.authorId)` attaches the full user repository result directly to each post, so any fields returned by `findById` beyond the intended public subset may leak in `GET /posts`. Prioritize checking for missing projection/allowlist serialization on the nested `author` object.

Confirmed API contract change: the endpoint now enriches every post with a new nested `author` object before `res.json(posts)`, changing the public response shape. Prioritize whether this requires explicit client coordination, versioning, or contract/public serialization tests that lock the exact shape and verify approved fields are present while unapproved fields are absent.

Unsafe mutation / entity-shaping risk: the code mutates the `posts` records returned from `db.posts.findAll()` in place by assigning `post.author`. If these are persistence-layer entities or shared model instances, this can cause unintended side effects in serialization or later reuse. Prioritize finding whether the handler should instead map posts into plain response objects with explicitly serialized author fields.

Confirmed N+1 query pattern in `src/routes/posts.ts` lines 8-10: after `db.posts.findAll()`, the loop performs one `db.users.findById(post.authorId)` per post, which will cause serial repeated user fetches and latency spikes as post counts grow. Prioritize checking for batching/eager-loading alternatives and ask for a test that asserts bounded database-call counts rather than only response timing.

Missing-author behavior is undefined at `src/routes/posts.ts` line 9: if `post.authorId` is stale or the user record does not exist, `post.author` may become `null`/`undefined` or trigger an error depending on repository behavior. Prioritize clarifying the intended contract (`author: null`, omitted field, or request failure) and ensure tests lock that behavior down.

Related-record reliability/performance edge case from duplicate author IDs in the loop at `src/routes/posts.ts` lines 8-10: multiple posts by the same author will still issue repeated identical `findById` calls, amplifying unnecessary database work even beyond the basic per-post lookup pattern. Prioritize checking whether repeated related-record fetches are deduplicated or otherwise batched.

iterations.final_synthesized_review_summary: 3 comments (High:2, Medium:1). `src/routes/posts.ts` has two confirmed high-impact issues in the `/posts` response path: it enriches posts with authors using a serialized N+1 lookup pattern, and it returns raw user repository objects directly in the A
iterations.evaluator_comments: Strong review: the N+1 performance issue and raw user-object exposure are well grounded, precisely located, and paired with concrete fixes plus solid regression tests. The async error-handling note is appropriately qualified and actionable. Minor deduction because the security finding depends on the repository/user shape and may be somewhat inferential without seeing the model/schema, and the review could mention whether mutating ORM/repository entities in-place (`post.author = ...`) has side effects depending on the data layer. Overall, this is accurate, practical, and comprehensive.
